items.new_candidate: Mrs. Wells (Thelma Crowder Wells, Port Gibson NAACP co-chair)
items.evidence: Guster names this person twice as the NAACP co-chair under chairperson Lily Mae Williams ('Lily Mae Williams was the chairperson. They had my wells. I know you remember her. She was the co-chair') and again as 'they were my Wales. That's what she did. It spoke. She could speak.' alongside Julia Jones and Jenny Eber Collins. The Mississippi Folklife 'Victory Day in Port Gibson' exhibit independently identifies Thelma Crowder Wells as a Port Gibson NAACP movement activist and NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware lawsuit defendant who 'overcame her initial fear to lead singing at rallies,' and photo-captions her as 'Mrs. Wells' standing beside Benjamin Hooks at the Victory Day celebration -- with Rev. Eddie Walls separately identified as the sign-holder. The two surnames in the original candidate ('Wells / Walls') reflect that both Mrs. Wells and Rev. Eddie Walls are documented Port Gibson movement figures; the female co-chair context here narrows the referent to Mrs. (Thelma Crowder) Wells. A LoC search-result snippet describing the Guster oral history likewise links Thelma Crowder Wells (b. 1908) to Port Gibson NAACP/Claiborne Hardware activism.

Same referent as 77.18 -- this is the third and fourth occurrence in the transcript of the same individual whose surname Whisper transcribed variably as 'my wells,' 'Miss Wales,' 'my Wales,' and 'my whales.' The 'Reverend Dawson stopped' context ('And Miss Wales. Yeah. Um, she talked about how Reverend Dawson stopped') matches Mrs. Wells's role as a respected speaker at mass meetings who commented on the church-discipline disputes; the later passage 'They were my whales, who was going to have a, uh, good paying check, because he was service-connected' describes Mrs. Wells's husband as having a service-connected (veteran's) benefit, consistent with a Port Gibson family. Mississippi Folklife establishes Thelma Crowder Wells as the Port Gibson NAACP 'movement stalwart' who led congregational singing at rallies. The 'Walls' alternative in the original candidate refers to Rev. Eddie Walls (a separately documented male Port Gibson activist), not this individual; the female co-chair / mass-meeting-speaker context throughout the transcript rules out Walls as the referent here.
